summary: The author secured 10th place by constructing a carefully diversified ensemble of three structural prediction models: TBM, Protenix, and RNAPro. The pipeline relied on iterative inference over the validation set, tracking both local and public leaderboard scores to finalize the submission. A key technical choice was chunking long RNA sequences for Protenix and toggling the `USE_RNA_MSA` flag to maximize prediction diversity across the five required submission slots. The author also identified and fixed a critical inference bottleneck that was preventing Protenix samples from being generated when TBM predictions met a threshold.
extensive_summary:
## Inference Pipeline & Model Allocation
The final submission allocated the five required prediction slots across three models: two for TBM, one for Protenix, and two for RNAPro. The pipeline was entirely inference-driven, focusing on refining and diversifying outputs rather than training new models.

## TBM Strategy
Template-Based Modeling served as the foundation. The author scanned up to 30 candidates using a global `PairwiseAligner`, ranking them by normalized alignment score and percent identity. Templates were adapted to query coordinates using alignment-aware copying with interpolation/extrapolation to preserve chain segments and stoichiometry. Predictions were generated by either directly copying the adapted template or applying a similarity-dependent Gaussian perturbation (with a fallback to geometric hinge/jitter for low similarity). Soft geometric smoothing via `adaptive_rna_constraints()` ensured local geometry remained realistic.

## Protenix & Long-Sequence Handling
Protenix predictions underwent strict quality control to reject collapsed or degenerate structures. To handle long RNAs, the author implemented a chunking strategy: sequences were split into overlapping segments (`MAX_SEQ_LEN ≈ 512`, `CHUNK_OVERLAP ≈ 64`), inferred independently, and seamlessly blended by aligning overlapping regions. This approach yielded consistent local validation improvements for specific long targets like `9MME` and `9ZCC`.

## RNAPro Refinement & Validation Tracking
RNAPro was used to refine TBM-derived predictions using MSA and RibonanzaNet2 embeddings, running 10 recycling cycles and 200 diffusion steps. The author meticulously tracked local validation (`validation_sequences.csv`) and Public LB scores across dozens of inference runs. A major breakthrough came from fixing a bottleneck in the inference script that incorrectly skipped Protenix generation when TBM predictions passed a threshold; resolving this immediately improved local validation by 0.02.

## Key Observations
The author noted that RNAPro performed exceptionally well locally but poorly on the Public LB. Enabling `USE_RNA_MSA` boosted local scores by ~0.03 but degraded LB performance when combined with TBM. Ultimately, the optimal strategy relied on model diversity (toggling `USE_RNA_MSA` for the two RNAPro slots) rather than heavy tuning or finetuning.
competition_flow: Inference was run iteratively on the 28 validation samples across TBM, Protenix, and RNAPro with varying parameters, followed by coordinate quality control, sequence chunking for long targets, and score tracking to finalize a diversified 5-prediction ensemble submission.
data_processing: Chunking long RNA sequences into overlapping segments (MAX_SEQ_LEN ≈ 512, CHUNK_OVERLAP ≈ 64) for Protenix
Coordinate quality control to reject collapsed/degenerate outputs and pad/repeat valid samples
Similarity-dependent Gaussian perturbation and geometric hinge/jitter for TBM
Aligning and blending overlapping Protenix segments for continuous backbone reconstruction
cv_strategy: Iterative inference on the 28 validation samples with meticulous tracking of local validation scores (validation_sequences.csv) and Public LB scores to guide parameter tuning and model allocation.
ensembling: Final submission used a 5-slot ensemble (2 TBM, 1 Protenix, 2 RNAPro) with diversity achieved by toggling the USE_RNA_MSA flag between true and false for the two RNAPro predictions, combined with coordinate QC and sequence chunking for long targets.
insights: Diversifying predictions across different models is more effective than generating multiple runs of the same non-deterministic model.
RNAPro performs significantly better on local validation than on the Public LB, indicating a potential distribution shift or evaluation metric mismatch.
Enabling RNA_MSA boosts local validation scores but can degrade Public LB performance when combined with TBM.
Fixing an inference bottleneck that blocked Protenix generation when TBM predictions met a threshold immediately improved local validation.
critical_findings: RNAPro's local validation performance was excellent but its Public LB score was poor, highlighting a critical validation-to-LB discrepancy.
Chunking long sequences only helped two specific validation targets (9MME, 9ZCC) but provided consistent local improvement, proving its value for long RNAs.
The Public LB appeared heavily dominated by TBM, causing many later notebooks to overlook Protenix integration due to an unaddressed inference bottleneck.
what_did_not_work: Reranking Protenix samples by model confidence consumed excessive GPU time for minimal score improvement.
Finetuning Protenix was attempted but abandoned due to time constraints and preprocessing complexity.
Using templates for Protenix yielded absolutely no difference in Public LB or local validation scores.
